message:
refactor(fcp-bootstrap): typed HardwareTokenEnrollmentNotImplemented variant (br-nh2xl)

The hardware-token bootstrap path ends at a provisioning handoff
step that is not yet wired (enrollment implementation lives in the
next bead in the 24llg.4.2.3 chain). Until now that refusal was
communicated via:

    BootstrapError::HardwareToken(format!(
        "certificate selected for {token_display} using {} key material, \
         but provisioning enrollment is not implemented yet",
        provisioning_material.pair.key.key_type
    ))

That's a stringly-typed refusal: callers wanting to distinguish
"not implemented yet" from real hardware-token errors had to
substring-match against the message, which breaks as soon as the
wording is touched.

Add a dedicated variant:

    HardwareTokenEnrollmentNotImplemented {
        token_display: String,
        key_material: String,
    }

with a thiserror `#[error(...)]` template that formats to
essentially the same operator-visible string, so log lines and
operator guides don't have to be re-parsed. The #[error] message
still highlights the token selection succeeded and only enrollment
is pending, which is the important signal for the operator (the
token is wired correctly).

Call-site update in BootstrapWorkflow: replace the
`BootstrapError::HardwareToken(format!(...))` branch in the
bootstrap flow with the typed variant, passing
`token_display.to_string()` and
`provisioning_material.pair.key.key_type.to_string()` through to
the struct fields. Existing HardwareToken(String) variant is
untouched and remains the home for all genuine hardware-token
errors.

Downstream matching code that wants to detect this specific
refusal can now do `matches!(err, BootstrapError::HardwareTokenEnrollmentNotImplemented { .. })`
instead of String comparisons.

Co-Authored-By: Claude <noreply@anthropic.com>
